fuzzy_prd: We need a small toolkit of stateless helper routines that a model-and-collection layer can use to talk to a backend API consistently on both server and client. The toolkit handles four concerns: (1) replacing named placeholders in a URL template with values from a record's attributes, and optionally cleaning up the placeholder keys from a parameter map so they don't get appended as redundant query parameters; (2) deeply comparing two objects or arrays to detect whether they differ in structure or content, checking both orderings; (3) building client-facing URLs by prepending a routing prefix to a resource path — with an option to pass through already-absolute URLs unchanged even when prefixing is requested; (4) translating a high-level persistence operation (read, create, update, delete) into either a transport-agnostic descriptor handed to a data adapter on the server side, or a call to the underlying HTTP transport on the client side. For the client side, when the caller supplies an error callback it should be wrapped so failures arrive as a normalized two-field object instead of raw XHR state — handle it the same way the server-side adapter normalizes errors before forwarding to downstream services.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: syncer.interpolateParams(model, url, params); syncer.objectsDiffer(a, b); syncer.serverSync(method, model, options); syncer.clientSync(method, model, options)

trigger_keywords: what is the default base segment for client URLs
what prefix is prepended when no apiPath is configured
what is the default routing prefix
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default base segment is '/api'. The separator marker is '/-'. A URL '/path/to/resource' with no config produces '/api/-/path/to/resource'. When apiPath is configured (e.g. '/foo/bar'), the result is '/foo/bar/-/path/to/resource'. When an optional api segment is provided it is inserted between base and separator: '/api/<segment>/-/path'.

trigger_keywords: how is an absolute URL detected for passthrough
what makes a URL treated as absolute
when is the routing prefix NOT prepended
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A URL is absolute when it contains '://' (the scheme separator). Absolute URLs are returned unchanged regardless of whether clientPrefix is true. This holds for URLs with ports like 'http://host:8080/path' — the ':8080' must not be mistaken for a path placeholder.

trigger_keywords: what HTTP method is used for read operation
what method does create map to
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Operation to HTTP method: read->GET, create->POST, update->PUT, delete->DELETE. The resource path is root joined with id (e.g. '/listings/0'). For read/delete, body is {}. For create/update, body contains the full record attributes including id.

trigger_keywords: how is a JSON content-type error response parsed
when is the error body parsed vs left as raw text
what triggers JSON parsing of the error body
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the failed response has a content-type header containing 'application/json', the responseText is parsed into a structured value and body becomes that parsed object (rendered as JSON object in output). Otherwise body remains the raw responseText string (rendered as a quoted JSON string). The two-field normalized object always has 'status' and 'body'.

trigger_keywords: what happens to consumed path parameters in the params map
are used placeholder keys removed from params
what keys remain in params after interpolation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any key in the auxiliary params map whose name was consumed as a path placeholder is deleted from that map. Keys not used as placeholders remain in the map. For example with url='/listings/:id' and params={'id':42,'foo':'bar'}, after interpolation params becomes {'foo':'bar'} and 'id' is removed.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle it the same way the server-side adapter normalizes errors before forwarding to downstream services
context_pointers.actual_meaning: syncer.clientSync in shared/syncer.js wraps the caller's error option with a function that calls Backbone.sync then constructs a normalized response object {status: xhr.status, body: <parsed or raw>} before invoking the original error callback.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. Operations: interpolate, objects_differ, format_client_url, get_url, server_sync, client_sync, client_sync_error.

feature1_interpolate_params.json.description: Substitute named placeholders in a URL template with values drawn from a record. A template segment of the form ':name' is replaced by the record's attribute of that name; every occurrence of a given placeholder is replaced. When an optional auxiliary parameter map is supplied, any key that was consumed as a path placeholder is removed from that map (so it will not later be appended as a redundant query parameter), while unrelated keys are left untouched. The interpolated URL is reported, and when a parameter map is supplied its remaining contents are reported as well.

feature2_objects_differ.json.description: Deeply compare two values and report whether they differ in structure or content. The comparison walks the union of keys present on either side; when both sides hold a nested container at the same key it recurses, otherwise it compares the two values for equality. Missing keys, differing scalar values, and order differences in sequences all count as a difference, while two structurally identical values (including nested containers and equal-length, same-order sequences) are reported as not differing. The comparison is evaluated in both orderings and each result is reported, demonstrating symmetry.

feature3_1_format_client_url.json.description: Build a client-facing URL for a resource path by prepending a routing prefix. By default the prefix is a fixed base segment, followed by a separator marker, then the resource path. When a named API segment is supplied, it is inserted between the base segment and the separator marker. The base segment may be overridden by a configured API path, in which case that configured value replaces the default base while the separator marker and resource path are still appended.

feature3_2_get_url_absolute.json.description: Resolve a URL while honoring already-absolute addresses. When the supplied address already contains a scheme separator ('://') it is treated as absolute and returned unchanged, even when client-side prefixing is requested. This holds for both common schemes and for absolute addresses that include an explicit port.

feature4_server_sync_request.json.description: Translate a server-side persistence operation on a record into a transport-agnostic request descriptor that is handed to the data adapter. The record has an id, a resource root path, an API name, and request headers. The descriptor carries: the HTTP method derived from the operation (read -> GET, create -> POST, update -> PUT, delete -> DELETE), the resource path (root plus the record id), a parsed query map, the supplied headers, the API name, and a body. For read/delete the body is empty; for create/update the body is the record's full serialized attributes.

feature5_1_client_sync_request.json.description: Translate a client-side persistence operation into a call to the underlying transport. The operation name and the record are forwarded unchanged, while the request options are rewritten so the URL is rewritten through client-side prefixing (a routing prefix is prepended to the resource path). The supplied headers are passed through. The reported result shows the forwarded operation name, the rewritten URL, and the headers seen by the transport.

feature5_2_client_error_response.json.description: Normalize a transport-level failure into a compact response object before handing it to the caller-supplied error callback. The failure carries a raw response body, an HTTP status code, and response headers. The wrapped handler produces an object with two fields: the status code and the body. By default the body is the raw response text; when the response advertises a JSON content type, the body is parsed into a structured value instead. The reported result shows the status and the resulting body (a quoted raw string when left unparsed, a structured object when parsed).
